summary: This is Amendment 0.1 EU-1 of Protocol IOV-MEL-202, a Phase 2, multicenter, open-label, single-arm study evaluating the efficacy and safety of the lifileucel treatment regimen in participants with previously treated advanced melanoma. The study targets patients who received one prior line of anti-PD-(L)1 therapy or progressed during/within 12 weeks after adjuvant anti-PD-(L)1 treatment, with optional prior BRAF ± MEK inhibitor therapy for BRAF V600 mutation-positive disease. Approximately 75 participants in the primary population (up to 100 overall) will be enrolled. The lifileucel regimen consists of nonmyeloablative lymphodepletion (cyclophosphamide and fludarabine), a single autologous TIL infusion, and abbreviated aldesleukin dosing. The primary endpoint is objective response rate per RECIST v1.1 by independent review committee. This EU-specific amendment addresses CTR Part 1 Assessment requests.

Synopsis
Protocol Title:

Brief Title:
A study of lifileucel (tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes) in adults with advanced melanoma
Regulatory Agency Identifier Numbers:
Rationale:
This is a Phase 2 study of the lifileucel treatment regimen in participants who previously received treatment for unresectable or metastatic (advanced) melanoma with 1 prior line of an anti-programmed cell death protein-1 (PD-1) or anti-programmed death ligand 1 (PD-L1) agent or whose melanoma progressed during or within ≤ 12 weeks after adjuvant anti-PD-(L)1 treatment (early relapse). Limiting prior therapy to 1 line of an anti-PD-(L)1 therapy with or without a line of therapy of proto-oncogene B-Raf (BRAF) ± mitogen-activated protein kinase (MEK) inhibitors will allow for assessment of treatment with the lifileucel regimen in less heavily treated and less heterogeneous patient population than was studied in the predecessor study, C-144-01. In Study C-144-01, the median number of prior systemic therapies for advanced melanoma was 3 with a range between 1 and 9 prior therapies (Chesney 2022). Use of lifileucel as an earlier line of treatment aligns with the United States (US) marketing approval and the recommendation in the US National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) guideline in this patient population.
Objectives and Endpoints:
The primary population includes (1 or 2) and (3 or 4):
Participants who previously received an anti-PD-(L)1 agent for advanced melanoma
Participants whose melanoma progressed during or within ≤ 12 weeks after adjuvant
anti-PD-(L)1 treatment (early relapse)
Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± a MEK inhibitor)
Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 wild-type
The overall population includes:
Participants in the primary population
Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and not previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± a MEK inhibitor)

Overall Design:
This is a Phase 2, open-label, single-arm, multicenter study of the lifileucel regimen in participants who previously received treatment for advanced melanoma with 1 prior line of a PD-(L)1 therapy or whose melanoma progressed during or within ≤ 12 weeks after adjuvant anti-PD-(L)1 treatment (early relapse). Participants who have BRAF V600 mutation positive melanoma may have received 1 additional prior line treatment with a BRAF inhibitor ± a MEK inhibitor or choose lifileucel as an alternative option to a BRAF inhibitor ± a MEK inhibitor. The lifileucel regimen consists of a preparative nonmyeloablative lymphodepletion (NMA-LD) regimen (ie, cyclophosphamide with mesna followed by fludarabine), the lifileucel infusion, and an abbreviated course of aldesleukin (interleukin-2). An IRC and an Independent Data Monitoring Committee (IDMC) will be appointed for this study (see the details below for the IDMC).
Brief Summary:
The purpose of this study is to investigate the efficacy and safety of the lifileucel regimen in participants with previously treated melanoma. The length of the study for each participant may be up to  after receiving lifileucel. After Screening, each participant will have surgery to remove a piece of the tumor that will be used to make the lifileucel, followed by a baseline visit prior to study treatment. Participants will then receive the lifileucel treatment regimen. This includes 2 drugs (cyclophosphamide and fludarabine) for		to decrease lymphocytes in the body, the lifileucel infusion (	), and up to	of aldesleukin to boost
the activity of the lifileucel. Study visits will be	, then every until  after the lifileucel infusion.
Number of Participants:
Approximately 75 participants are planned to be infused with lifileucel in the primary population with approximately 100 participants in the overall population.
The prospectively defined hypothesis H1 and H2 will be tested sequentially for the primary endpoint of ORR:
Primary Hypothesis (H1): H01: ORR ≤10% vs. Ha1: ORR >10% in the primary population
Secondary Hypothesis (H2): H02: ORR ≤10% vs. Ha2: ORR >10% in the overall population
At a two-sided overall significance level of 0.05 using the exact test, for H1, a sample size of 75 participants will provide approximately 99% power to reject null hypothesis of 10% ORR assuming the true ORR for lifileucel is 31% in participants in primary population
(Chesney 2022); similarly for H2, a sample size of 100 will provide more than 99% power to reject the null hypothesis of 10% ORR assuming the true ORR for lifileucel is 31% in the overall population.

INTRODUCTION
Lifileucel is a preparation of autologous TIL that is in development for the immunotherapy of solid tumors. The product is derived from tumor-infiltrating CD45+CD3+ lymphocytes obtained from resected tumor samples, and as such, each dose is patient specific. This protocol will evaluate the safety and efficacy of the lifileucel regimen in participants with advanced melanoma.
Study Rationale
This is a Phase 2 study of the lifileucel treatment regimen in participants who previously received treatment for unresectable or metastatic (advanced) melanoma with 1 prior line of a PD-(L)1 therapy or whose melanoma progressed during or within ≤ 12 weeks after adjuvant anti-PD-(L)1 treatment (early relapse). Limiting prior therapy to 1 line of an anti-PD-(L)1 therapy with or without a line of therapy of BRAF ± MEK inhibitors will allow for assessment of treatment with the lifileucel regimen in less heavily treated and less heterogeneous patient population than was studied in the predecessor study, C-144-01. In Study C-144-01, the median number of prior systemic therapies for advanced melanoma was 3 with a range between 1 and 9 prior therapies (Chesney 2022). Use of lifileucel as an earlier line of treatment aligns with the US marketing approval and the recommendation in the US NCCN guideline in this patient population.
Background
Advanced (unresectable or metastatic) melanoma is a life-threatening condition that is difficult to treat, and despite recent advances in treatment, there remains a significant unmet medical need.
While in situ and locally invasive melanomas are curable by surgery, metastatic disease is difficult to treat and remains a significant public health concern (Steininger 2021). Despite advances in front-line systemic treatments that include anti-PD-1 monotherapy (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) or the combination of nivolumab and ipilimumab (anti-cytotoxic T lymphocyte antigen-4 [CTLA-4]) or relatlimab (anti-LAG-3) as well as therapies targeting driver mutations in the BRAF pathway for tumors with these mutations (Keilholz 2020; Dummer 2015;
Tawbi 2022), the majority of patients do not achieve long-term benefit from these therapies (Larkin 2015; Luke 2017; Gide 2018; Long 2022). Patients progressing after treatment with immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) (including combination therapies) and/or targeted agents have limited options, with currently available therapies resulting in few responses and fewer durable responses (Goldinger 2022; Buchbinder 2019; Weichenthal 2019).
Lifileucel is an autologous T-cell immunotherapy based on the manufacturing process initially developed at the US National Cancer Institute (NCI) by Rosenberg et al and further optimized by Iovance. The product is composed of autologous TIL, which are obtained from an individual patient’s resected tumor and expanded ex vivo through cell culture.
The lifileucel treatment regimen has demonstrated meaningful clinical responses and a manageable safety profile in clinical studies across several tumor types (Jazaeri 2019; O’Malley 2021; Chesney 2022; Sarnaik 2022; Medina 2023; Schoenfeld 2024) and is based on almost
3 decades of Phase 1/2 studies of TIL-based adoptive cell therapies (Rosenberg 2011). The
NMA-LD and aldesleukin are included in the regimen to support T-cell activity, expansion, and activation of the transferred TIL (Dudley 2002a; Dudley 2002b; Klebanoff 2005). These additional agents do not have antitumor effects on their own under the conditions of their administration within the lifileucel regimen (Goff 2016).
Lifileucel (Amtagvi™) received its first marketing approval in in February 2024 from the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of adult patients with unresectable or metastatic melanoma previously treated with a PD-1 blocking antibody, and if BRAF V600 mutation positive, a BRAF inhibitor ± a MEK inhibitor. This approval was based on Study C-144-01, a Phase 2, global, multicenter, multicohort, open-label, single-arm clinical trial
evaluating the efficacy and safety of treatment with lifileucel in adult patients with unresectable or metastatic melanoma. The patient population in Study C-144-1 had melanoma that was heavily pretreated and had progressed on multiple standard therapies (median of 3.0 and a maximum of 9 prior lines of systemic therapies), including ICIs (ie, anti-PD-(L)1 ± anti-CTLA-4 therapies) and targeted therapies such as BRAF ± MEK inhibitors, if BRAF V600-mutated.
The US NCCN guidelines (version 2.2025) for metastatic or unresectable melanoma list the following preferred second-line or subsequent therapy agents to be sequenced after first-line therapy with either anti-PD-1 containing regimens or combination targeted therapy with a BRAF inhibitor regimen if BRAF V600 mutation positive, with the caveat that re-challenge with a therapy from the same therapeutic class is unlikely to be successful:
PD-1 blocking antibodies (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) or combination therapy with PD-1 blocking antibodies/CTLA-4 blocking antibodies (ipilimumab) or nivolumab/LAG-3 blocking antibodies (relatlimab)
If BRAF mutation-positive, BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib, vemurafenib, encorafenib) in combination MEK inhibitors (trametinib, cobimetinib, binimetinib)
TIL therapy with lifileucel
Study IOV-MEL-202 will study the lifileucel treatment regimen in a less heavily treated and less heterogeneous patient population than Study C-144-01 by limiting prior therapy to 1 line of an anti-PD-(L)1 therapy and limiting the lines of therapy prior to lifileucel to only those that are recommended within the NCCN guidelines. Because outcomes to TIL therapy in patients who have disease refractory to BRAF inhibitor ± a MEK inhibitor have been reported to be worse than in those who are naive to BRAF and MEK inhibitors (Seitter 2021), treatment of participants with melanoma that has the BRAF V600 mutation with prior BRAF ± MEK inhibitors is optional. Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and naïve to BRAF ± MEK inhibitors will be included in the overall population but not in the primary population for analysis.
A detailed description of the chemistry, pharmacology, efficacy, and safety of lifileucel is provided in the Investigator’s Brochure (IB).
Benefit/Risk Assessment
More detailed information about the known and expected benefits and risks and reasonably expected AEs of lifileucel may be found in the IB.
Risk Assessment
Safety of Lifileucel Therapy
Overall, toxicities and AEs observed during treatment with TIL cellular therapy have been associated primarily with either the NMA-LD preconditioning regimen or the aldesleukin doses administered after the TIL infusion. Few infusion-related AEs have been documented following the TIL infusion itself, with Grade ≥ 3 events rarely observed (Rosenberg 2011; Goff 2016; Radvanyi 2012; Dudley 2008; Pilon-Thomas 2012; Sarnaik 2017a; Sarnaik 2017b; Besser 2009;
Besser 2010; Ellebaek 2012; Andersen 2016).
Resection of a sample of the tumor(s) for manufacturing the lifileucel product is associated with risks inherent with the surgical procedure and administration of anesthesia.
Benefit Assessment
The potential benefit to a participant includes receiving study intervention that may cease, slow, or reverse the progression of melanoma.
Patients with BRAF V600 mutation-positive melanoma who have not received prior targeted therapy have the option for treatment with BRAF and MEK inhibitors administered as second-line therapy with potential for ORRs of 48% to 58% (Ascierto 2023; Atkins 2023). However, BRAF and MEK inhibitor combinations provide modest DORs, with a median DOR of
14.2 months based on a recent update of one of the trials (Atkins 2025). These participants will be informed of the availability of this treatment option during the informed consent process for this study.
Potential benefit from lifileucel treatment for these patients is supported by Study C-144-01, which demonstrated similar treatment effects of lifileucel in participants with BRAF wildtype and BRAF V600 mutation-positive melanoma previously treated with targeted therapy.
Importantly, the median DOR in the BRAF V600 mutation-positive subgroup was 36.5 months (95% confidence interval [CI]: 4.8, not reached), which is consistent with that observed in the overall population (median DOR of 36.5 months, 95% CI: 8.3, not reached). Other TIL studies further support the consistency of treatment effects with TIL in patients with BRAF wildtype and BRAF V600 mutation-positive melanoma (Fernández 2024).
Outcomes to TIL therapy in patients who have disease refractory to a BRAF inhibitor ± MEK inhibitor have been reported to be worse than in those who are naive to BRAF and MEK inhibitors (Seitter 2021).
These data support potential benefit from use of lifileucel therapy for patients with BRAF V600 mutation-positive melanoma prior to BRAF ± MEK inhibitor treatment, and this study is designed to generate initial data on this. Treatment with lifileucel is appropriate for patients who choose IOV-MEL-202 study therapy over treatment with a BRAF inhibitor ± MEK inhibitor after having been informed about other therapy options which may include BRAF ± MEK inhibitor, chemotherapy, other experimental therapies, or supportive care.
Overall Benefit Risk Conclusion
Considering the following, the benefit-risk profile of the lifileucel treatment regimen is appropriate for the participants planned for inclusion in this study:
The known toxicities for the lifileucel regimen (ie, NMA-LD, aldesleukin, and lifileucel) can be monitored and managed
The measures taken to minimize risk to participants
The lack of effective treatment options that provide durable benefit for the study population
This study includes participants independent of sex. Enrollment is limited to adults since the safety and efficacy of the lifileucel regimen have not been established in pediatric patients.
In summary, the lifileucel treatment regimen represents a one-time treatment, with the possibility of offering a durable treatment option for patients with advanced melanoma who have limited treatment options after the failure of front-line therapy (ie, anti-PD-(L)1-containing therapy).
OBJECTIVES AND ENDPOINTS
The primary population includes (1 or 2) and (3 or 4):
Participants who previously received an anti-PD-(L)1 agent for advanced melanoma
Participants whose melanoma progressed during or within ≤ 12 weeks after adjuvant anti-PD-(L)1 treatment (early relapse)
Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± a MEK inhibitor)
Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 wild-type The overall population includes:
Participants in the primary population
Participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and not previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± a MEK inhibitor)

STUDY DESIGN
Overall Design
This is a Phase 2, open-label, single-arm, multicenter study of the lifileucel regimen in participants who previously received treatment for advanced melanoma with 1 prior line of a PD-(L)1 therapy or whose melanoma progressed during or within ≤ 12 weeks after adjuvant anti-PD-(L)1 treatment (early relapse). Participants who have BRAF V600 mutation positive melanoma may have received 1 additional prior line treatment with a BRAF inhibitor ± a MEK inhibitor.
The lifileucel regimen consists of a preparative NMA-LD regimen (ie, cyclophosphamide with mesna followed by fludarabine), the lifileucel infusion, and an abbreviated course of aldesleukin. An IRC and an IDMC have been appointed for this study (Section 10.1.6).
Screening
The most recently approved ICF must be signed before any study-related assessments are performed and may be obtained outside of the	window prior to the tumor resection (Section 10.1.3).
Screening assessments, unless otherwise indicated, must be performed within prior to the tumor resection.
A documented review of the Participant Eligibility Form by the sponsor’s medical monitor or designee is required prior to tumor resection.
Participants who failed the initial screening attempt and those who meet the inclusion criteria, but do not receive lifileucel for any reason (eg, due to an AE or because lifileucel is not available), may be rescreened after consultation with the medical monitor (Section 5.4). Participants who failed the initial screening attempt who are rescreened will be assigned a new participant number. Participants who met eligibility criteria but did not receive lifileucel may be rescreened using the original participant number.
Tumor resection must occur within	of completion of the screening assessments.
The Screening activities include:
Demographic information will be recorded (Section 8.1.1).
Relevant and significant medical/surgical history and concurrent illnesses will be collected for all participants.
The participant’s cancer history will be recorded (Section 8.1.2).
A comprehensive physical examination, including recording of height and weight,
will be performed (Section 8.3.1).
Participants with a history of uveitis must have an eye examination performed by a trained eye specialist to rule out active uveitis that requires treatment (Section 8.3.1).
Vital signs will be measured (Section 8.3.2).
ECOG performance status will be assessed (Section 8.3.3).
A 12-lead ECG (Section 8.3.4) and other cardiac evaluations (Section 8.3.5) will be performed.
Pulmonary function tests (Section 8.3.6) will be performed; evaluations completed within  prior to signing the ICF will be accepted.
Hematology, coagulation, clinical chemistry, liver tests, thyroid tests, routine urinalysis, and measured or estimated creatinine clearance (CrCl) laboratory tests will be performed (Section 8.3.7 and Appendix 2 [Section 10.2]).
A serum pregnancy test for participants of CBP will be performed (Section 8.3.8).
Infectious disease testing will be performed (Section 8.3.9 and Appendix 2 [Section 10.2]).
Participants will be evaluated for evidence of any uncontrolled or active systemic infection. Prophylactic anti-infectious therapy is acceptable. See Section 6.9.2.1 for more details regarding treatment of an active infection prior to the start of NMA-LD.
Radiographic assessments will be performed (Section 8.2.1).
All medications, therapies, or interventions received by the participant starting prior to Screening must be recorded (Section 6.9).
Medical occurrences that begin after obtaining informed consent but before tumor resection will be recorded as pretreatment medical events on the Medical History eCRF, not as AEs (Section 8.4.1).
Tumor Resection (Enrollment)
The date of enrollment is the tumor resection date. Participants who do not undergo tumor resection or for whom a tumor resection was attempted but not done may be rescreened following consultation with the medical monitor.
The following will be performed prior to the tumor resection:
A serum or urine pregnancy test for participants of CBP, within	of the tumor resection (Section 8.3.8)
Infectious disease testing is required on the day of tumor resection or within  after tumor resection for participants whose tumor samples are acquired in the European Union, United Kingdom, or other regions where required (Section 8.3.9).
Details of the tumor resection are provided in Section 6.1.1. Tumor tissue samples (FFPE and viable, see Section 8.8.1.1 and Section 8.8.1.2) will be obtained for biomarker analysis and research experiments (Section 8.8).
Additional activities to take place on the day of the tumor resection include:
Participants will be evaluated for evidence of any uncontrolled or active systemic infection (Section 6.9.2.1). Prophylactic anti-infectious therapy is acceptable (Section 6.9.2.2).
All medications, therapies, or interventions, including those that are part of the tumor resection procedure, received by the participant must be recorded (Section 6.9).
Medical occurrences that begin after obtaining informed consent and are unrelated to the tumor resection will be recorded as pretreatment medical events on the Medical History eCRF, not as AEs (Section 8.4.1).
All AEs related to the tumor resection will be assessed as per the National Cancer Institute-Common Terminology Criteria for Adverse Events (NCI-CTCAE) (Section 8.4 and Appendix 3 [Section 10.3]).
Blood samples will be obtained for immune monitoring (Section 8.8.2).
The details of any hospitalizations through  will be recorded.
Baseline
This period includes the performance of the baseline activities following the tumor resection and  prior to the lifileucel administration. During this period, the resected tumor materials received at the manufacturing site are utilized for lifileucel generation.
The baseline activities include:
The participant should complete the HRQoL questionnaires before any other procedures commence. The EQ-5D-5L should be completed first, followed by the EORTC QLQ-C30 (Section 8.11).
A symptom-directed physical examination will be performed (Section 8.3.1).
Vital signs will be measured (Section 8.3.2).
ECOG performance status will be assessed (Section 8.3.3).
A 12-lead ECG, if clinically indicated, will be performed (Section 8.3.4).
If the investigator determines that the participant's cardiopulmonary status has deteriorated since the most recent echocardiogram (ECHO)/multigated acquisition scan (MUGA)/ magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), an ECHO/MUGA/MRI will be repeated within  prior to initiation of NMA-LD and the results will be documented on the Health Status Reconfirmation form (Section 8.3.5).
Spirometry also should be performed within  prior to NMA-LD as needed for participants meeting any of the criteria listed in Section 8.3.6 or has a new pulmonary AE or new or worsening pulmonary symptoms since Screening.
Hematology, clinical chemistry, liver tests, and measured or estimated CrCl laboratory tests will be performed, and thyroid tests are required if abnormal at Screening or if clinically indicated (Section 8.3.7 and Appendix 2 [Section 10.2]).
A serum or urine pregnancy test for participants of CBP will be performed (Section 8.3.8).
Participants will be evaluated for evidence of any uncontrolled or active systemic infection and will not be allowed to proceed to NMA-LD if an infection requiring
treatment is found. Prophylactic anti-infectious therapy is acceptable
(Section 6.9.2.2). See Section 6.9.2.1 for more details regarding treatment of an active infection prior to the start of NMA-LD.
Baseline imaging must be obtained post-tumor resection for lifileucel generation and before any study therapy is initiated, including NMA-LD (Section 8.2.1). If bridging therapy is administered, baseline imaging should be done after completion of the bridging therapy. Repeat baseline brain imaging is required for participants with a history of brain metastases or if clinically indicated.
All medications, therapies, or interventions received by the participant must be recorded (Section 6.9).
Medical occurrences that begin before the start of NMA-LD but after obtaining informed consent and are related to performance of the screening or baseline assessments but are unrelated to the tumor resection will be recorded as pretreatment medical events on the Medical History eCRF, not as AEs (Section 8.4.1).
All AEs related to the tumor resection will be assessed as per the NCI-CTCAE (Section 8.4 and Appendix 3 [Section 10.3]).
The details of any hospitalizations through  will be recorded. When the results of the baseline assessment as described above are available:
The investigator will assess whether the participant has had any clinical deterioration or new medical findings that would put the participant at increased risk when receiving the NMA-LD, lifileucel, and aldesleukin administrations.
If the participant continues to be stable to proceed with administration of the lifileucel regimen (NMA-LD, lifileucel, and aldesleukin), this will be documented on the Health Status Reconfirmation form, which must be sent to the sponsor or designee for review (Sections 6.1.2 and 8.3.10).
If the participant will not proceed with administration of NMA-LD for any reason (Section 7.1), the participant should be monitored for pretreatment medical events and AEs related to the tumor resection  . After this period, the disposition information as required at the End-of-assessment Visit (see
Section 4.1.5.1) and End-of-study Visit (see Section 4.1.6) will be collected and the participant will exit the study.
The Authorization to Receive Lymphodepletion form will be completed by the appropriate site staff and sent to the sponsor or designee for review (Sections 6.1.2 and 8.3.10).
Treatment Period
After the sponsor medical monitor or designee verifies that the criteria for lymphodepletion authorization are met, the completed Authorization to Receive Lymphodepletion form will be returned to the site.
Receipt of both the Health Status Reconfirmation form and the Authorization to Receive Lymphodepletion form at the site from the sponsor medical monitor or designee is required prior to the initiation of the NMA-LD regimen.
Then, based on institutional standards and individual participant risk/benefit assessment, the investigator may choose to proceed with initiation of the NMA-LD at the earliest during shipment of the manufactured lifileucel product to the clinical site.
Each participant will receive study intervention:
NMA-LD (see Section 6.1.2)
Lifileucel infusion (see Section 6.1.3)
Aldesleukin administration (see Section 6.1.4)
If a participant begins NMA-LD but does not, for any reason, receive lifileucel, the participant should be monitored  for safety (Section 8.4.1) and then an End-of-treatment Visit (Section 4.1.4.4) should be performed. In addition, the disposition information as required at the End-of-assessment Visit (Section 4.1.5.1) and End-of-study Visit (Section 4.1.6) should be collected.
Setting Required for the Administration of the Lifileucel Regimen
Per institutional or local standards, participants may be treated in a hospital setting during administration of NMA-LD, lifileucel, and/or aldesleukin, or longer as per institutional standards.
In certain regions where this is required by local or institutional standards (eg, France), participants should be hospitalized from the initiation of NMA-LD until completion of the aldesleukin treatment and recovery from any serious side effects associated with the lifileucel regimen.
Assessments Performed Prior to Administration of a Component of the Lifileucel Regimen on Dosing Days

Scientific Rationale for Study Design
This single-arm study is designed to confirm the results from the predecessor study, C-144-01 but in a more homogenous patient population with respect to the number of prior anticancer therapies (see Section 2.2). Although this study is designed without a control group, any tumor regressions observed during the study can be attributed to the study intervention, given the nature of the participants’ advanced disease, requirement for progression on prior PD-L(1) therapy for all participants, and the fact that the study intervention (the lifileucel regimen) is administered as one-time monotherapy.
The primary efficacy endpoint for the study, ORR per RECIST v1.1, is a validated method for evaluating tumor response in a single-arm study.
Justification for Dose
The lifileucel regimen (Table 3) consists of:
The preparative NMA-LD regimen (ie, cyclophosphamide with mesna followed by fludarabine)
The lifileucel infusion
An abbreviated course of aldesleukin following the lifileucel infusion
The preparative NMA-LD regimen in this study includes doses of cyclophosphamide and fludarabine that are higher than those used in chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy but are lower than those used in hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (Betof Warner 2024).
The lifileucel dose range in this study (1.8 × 109 to 100 × 109 total viable cells) is based on the minimum and maximum values determined in a statistical analysis of lifileucel batches generated in Study C-144-01 Cohorts 2 and 4, with statistical outliers excluded (Table 2).
Table 2	Statistical Analysis for Potency: Total Viable Cells
Abbreviations: GCV = geometric coefficient of variation
The lifileucel treatment regimen has demonstrated meaningful clinical responses and a manageable safety profile in clinical studies across several tumor types (Jazaeri 2019; O’Malley 2021; Chesney 2022; Sarnaik 2022; Medina 2023; Schoenfeld 2024) and is based on almost
3 decades of Phase 1/2 studies of TIL-based adoptive cell therapies (Rosenberg 2011). The NMA-LD and aldesleukin are included in the regimen to support T-cell activity, expansion, and activation of the transferred TIL (Dudley 2002a; Dudley 2002b; Klebanoff 2005). These additional agents do not have antitumor effects on their own under the conditions of their administration within the lifileucel regimen (Goff 2016).

Tumor Resection for Lifileucel Generation
At least 1 resectable lesion (or aggregate of lesions) is required for lifileucel generation. If the participant has only one identified target lesion at Screening, another lesion (or aggregate of lesions) must be identified and resected for lifileucel generation.
It is preferred that the tumor planned for resection has not been previously irradiated. If it has, then the irradiation must have been completed at least  prior to tumor resection and the irradiated lesions must have unequivocal radiographically demonstrated progression.
It is encouraged that tumor tissue be obtained from multiple and diverse metastatic lesions, as long as the surgical resection does not pose additional risks to the participant and does not compromise the number of target lesions for disease assessments.
It is imperative that only the participant’s own (autologous) study treatment (lifileucel) be administered to the same individual participant. For this reason, the tumor sample for lifileucel generation must be procured and handled to ensure the appropriate identification of the study product at all times.
Refer to the Tumor Procurement and Shipping Manual for details on the preparation and handling of the tumor specimens.
Participants who had a tumor resection but did not receive lifileucel generated from the initial/prior tumor sample, or for whom lifileucel could not be generated from the initial/prior resection due to any reason, can be re-resected, if necessary, and infused if all inclusion and exclusion criteria are met.
Information on the tumor resections for the biomarker analyses and research experiments can be found in Section 8.8.1.

Preparation of Cyclophosphamide
Refer to the cyclophosphamide current approved local prescribing information for instructions on dose preparation.
For consistency in dosing, actual weight (or per institutional practice) should be used to calculate the dose for all participants (including participants with BMI ≥ 35.0 kg/m2).
In addition to the amount of fluid needed to reconstitute and dilute the cyclophosphamide, 1 L of fluids and mesna (eg, IV normal saline) may be administered with each cyclophosphamide dose to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis. Excess volumes of supplemental fluid beyond 1 L may not be needed at the dose of cyclophosphamide administered and should be avoided if possible (Hamadani 2012). Daily monitoring of body weight and fluid input and output volumes during NMA-LD up to the day after the last dose of aldesleukin should be performed to ensure that volume overload does not occur, and IV fluids or diuretics should be adjusted per institutional standards to ensure minimal accumulation of excess fluids that may lead to pulmonary edema while ensuring adequate urine output to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis.
For more information, refer to the cyclophosphamide current approved local prescribing information.
Preparation of Mesna
Mesna is administered to reduce the risk of hemorrhagic cystitis related to cyclophosphamide administration.
The total dose of mesna may be adjusted per institutional standards if the amount of cyclophosphamide is reduced. It is preferred that mesna be administered orally if feasible. If mesna is to be administered IV, dilute the volume of mesna injection or infusion per institutional standard.
For more information, refer to the current approved local prescribing information for mesna.
Preparation of Fludarabine
Refer to the fludarabine current approved local prescribing information for instructions on dose preparation. For consistency in dosing, actual weight (or per institutional practice) should be used to calculate the dose for all participants (including participants with BMI ≥ 35.0 kg/m2).

Overdose is not applicable for lifileucel. In the event of cyclophosphamide, mesna, fludarabine, or aldesleukin overdose, refer to the current approved local prescribing information for details on management.
In the event of an overdose, the investigator should:
Evaluate the participant to determine, in consultation with the medical monitor, if possible, whether study intervention should be interrupted or whether the dose should be reduced
Closely monitor the participant for any AE/SAE and laboratory abnormalities as medically appropriate
Document the quantity of the excess dose as well as the duration of the overdose An overdose should be reported as a Special Situation (Section 10.3.5).

Active Infection Prior to NMA-LD
Participants with evidence of any uncontrolled or active systemic infection requiring ongoing treatment cannot proceed to NMA-LD. Prophylactic anti-infectious therapy is acceptable.
Those with herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection should receive treatment doses of anti-HSV medications as per institutional standards until documented resolution. Similarly, participants with clinically significant cytomegalovirus (CMV) viremia detected on NAAT should receive anti-CMV therapy per institutional standards until resolution of viremia. Full resolution of infection should be documented by clinical evaluation and/or NAAT prior to the start of the NMA-LD preparative regimen.

Antibacterial Infection Prophylaxis
Pre-existing bacterial infections should be adequately treated to resolution prior to the start of the NMA-LD, lifileucel, or aldesleukin administration.
Following NMA-LD, participants will receive antibacterial prophylaxis with fluoroquinolone (or substituted with an alternative antibiotic per institutional standards for participants who are intolerant to fluoroquinolone) from the start of neutropenia until the resolution of the neutropenia, at a minimum, in addition to any institutional standards.
Treatment with aldesleukin is associated with impaired neutrophil function (reduced chemotaxis) and an increased risk of disseminated infection, including sepsis and bacterial endocarditis.
Participants with indwelling central lines are particularly at risk for infection with Gram-positive microorganisms during aldesleukin treatment. Antibiotic prophylaxis with oxacillin, nafcillin, ciprofloxacin, or vancomycin has been associated with a reduced incidence of staphylococcal infections, and coverage for staphylococcus should be added to the participant’s antibacterial infection prophylaxis if the participant is not receiving any other antibiotics covering staphylococcal infections with choice of antibiotic based on local antibiotic resistance patterns and renal function.
Infection Management
The infection risk of participants treated with the lifileucel regimen is comparable to participants treated with hematopoietic cell transplants or other cell therapies, necessitating strict monitoring and early initiation of broad treatment for infections.
Treatment of Febrile Neutropenia
Participants are expected to become neutropenic following the NMA-LD preparative regimen. Furthermore, aldesleukin causes neutrophil migration dysfunction, putting participants at significant risk for bacteremia and sepsis including Pseudomonas infections. For any documented fevers, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotic with adequate Pseudomonas coverage is necessary as per institutional standards and local antibiogram. Aminoglycosides should be avoided unless there is clear evidence of worsening sepsis. Consider broad-spectrum antibiotics for both gram negative and positive organisms until an infectious organism is identified.
After febrile neutropenia, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics should continue until the neutrophil count is  even if no bloodstream infectious agent is identified. If a bloodstream agent is identified, the antibiotic may be tailored to treat the infection as per institutional standard of care. Infectious disease consultation will be obtained for all participants with unexplained fever, any infectious complications, or as per standard of care at the treating institution.
Serious suspected infections in the participant within  of NMA-LD with neutrophil count should also be treated empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics for gram
negative and positive organisms until the participant has recovered or an organism has been identified because the reconstituting immune system may not be sufficient during this time. An infectious disease specialist should be consulted as clinically indicated for persistent signs of infection.
Treatment of Sepsis
Sepsis can mimic aldesleukin side effects. Fever may be masked during aldesleukin administration due to scheduled indomethacin and acetaminophen or equivalent. Neutropenic participants exhibiting hypotension or oliguria unresponsive to IV fluids should be evaluated for infection and broad-spectrum antibiotics should be initiated.

Palliative RT is permitted between the time of tumor resection and initiation of the NMA-LD preparative regimen if done prior to baseline imaging and if it does not impact the availability of measurable lesion(s) for the tumor response assessments and after discussion with the medical monitor. DISCONTINUATION OF STUDY INTERVENTION AND PARTICIPANT DISCONTINUATION/WITHDRAWAL
Discontinuation of specific sites or of the study as a whole is detailed in Section 10.1.10.
Non-initiation or Discontinuation of Study Intervention
A participant who had tumor resection will not initiate the NMA-LD regimen if any of the following criteria are met:
Participant is not stable enough to proceed to administration of the lifileucel regimen (NMA-LD, lifileucel, and aldesleukin) after tumor resection based on baseline assessments and the investigator’s clinical judgement (see Section 6.1.2).
The participant may be reassessed for treatment after discussion with the medical monitor.
Autologous lifileucel product is unavailable (see Section 6.1.1).
The participant may be considered for another resection after discussion with the medical monitor.
Any of the study discontinuation criteria in Section 7.2.1 are met.
A participant will discontinue the lifileucel regimen (NMA-LD, lifileucel, or aldesleukin) early if any of the following criteria are met (discussion with the medical monitor is encouraged prior to discontinuing the lifileucel regimen):
Lifileucel product cannot be administered (see Section 6.1.1).
Grade ≥ 3 drug-related immune AEs that involve vital organs (heart, kidneys, brain, eye, liver, colon, adrenal gland, lungs) with symptoms emerging following the lifileucel infusion
Grade ≥ 3 allergic reactions including bronchospasm or generalized urticaria that do not, in the opinion of the investigator, resolve after optimal medical management
Meeting criteria for permanent discontinuation of aldesleukin treatment (Appendix 7 [Section 10.7])
Determination by the investigator that continued study intervention is not in the best interest of the participant
Administration of prohibited concomitant medication (see Section 6.9.1.3)
Initiation of a new anticancer therapy (see Section 6.9.1.3)
Pregnancy (see Section 8.3.8)
Withdrawal of consent to treatment (partial withdrawal of consent, see Section 7.2.2)
Any of the study discontinuation criteria in Section 7.2.1 are met

Full Withdrawal of Consent
A participant may withdraw from the study at any time at the participant’s own request for any reason (or without providing a reason).
In the event of a participant’s full withdrawal of consent, the investigator will promptly notify the medical monitor. If the participant withdraws full consent to participate in the study, no additional data are to be collected.
The participant will be permanently discontinued from the study intervention and the study at that time.
If the participant withdraws consent for disclosure of future information, the sponsor may retain and continue to use any data collected before such a withdrawal of consent.
If a participant withdraws from the study, the participant may request destruction of any samples taken and not tested, and the investigator must document this in the site study records.
Lost to Follow up
A participant will be considered lost to follow-up if the participant repeatedly fails to return for scheduled visits and is unable to be contacted by the study site.
The following actions must be taken if a participant fails to return to the clinic for a required study visit or is unavailable for a required Long-term Follow-up contact:
The site must attempt to contact the participant and reschedule the missed visit as soon as possible, counsel the participant on the importance of maintaining the assigned visit schedule, and ascertain whether the participant wishes to and/or should continue in the study.
Before a participant is deemed lost to follow-up, the investigator or designee must make every effort to regain contact with the participant (where possible, 3 telephone calls, and if necessary, a certified letter to the participant’s last known mailing address or local equivalent methods). These contact attempts should be documented in the participant’s medical record.
Should the participant continue to be unreachable, the participant will be considered to have withdrawn from the study.
Site personnel, or an independent third party, may attempt to collect the vital status of the participant within legal and ethical boundaries, including those who did not get study intervention. Public sources may be searched for vital status information. If vital status is determined as deceased, this will be documented and the participant will not be considered lost to follow-up. Sponsor personnel will not be involved in any attempts to collect vital status information.
STUDY ASSESSMENTS AND PROCEDURES
Study procedures and their timing are summarized in the Schedule of Activities (Section 1.3). Protocol waivers or exemptions are not allowed.
Adherence to the study design requirements, including those specified in the Schedule of Activities, is essential and required for study conduct.
All screening evaluations must be completed and reviewed to confirm that potential participants meet all eligibility criteria. The investigator will maintain a screening log to record details of all participants screened and to confirm eligibility or record reasons for screening failure, as applicable.
Procedures conducted as part of the participant’s routine clinical management (eg, blood count) and obtained before signing of the ICF may be utilized for screening or baseline purposes provided the procedures met the protocol-specified criteria and were performed within the timeframe defined in the Schedule of Activities.
In the event of a significant study-continuity issue (eg, caused by a pandemic), alternate strategies for participant visits, assessments, medication distribution, and monitoring may be implemented by the sponsor or the investigator, as per local health authority/ethics requirements.
Administrative and General/Baseline Procedures
Demographic Information
The demographic information to be collected includes the year of birth, age, sex, and race/ethnic origin.

Relevant and significant medical/surgical history and concurrent illnesses will be recorded.

The participant must have documented diagnosis of the malignancy under study via a pathology report.
Efficacy Assessments
Planned time points for all efficacy assessments are provided in the Schedule of Activities (Section 1.3).
Radiographic Assessments
Radiographic assessments will occur at the following time points:
Screening: Imaging is required to ensure eligibility. Participants do not need to repeat images if they have historical media performed within  prior to signing of the ICF that confirms progression of disease on or after the most recent therapy and on
which prospective target lesions can be identified for RECIST v1.1 measurements. Screening imaging must include computed tomography (CT) scans of the chest, abdomen, pelvis (MRI of abdomen and pelvis is also allowed), and any relevant anatomic regions based on disease history, signs, and symptoms of each participant. A screening CT or MRI of the brain is required for all participants.
Baseline: Baseline imaging (CT scans of chest, abdomen, pelvis, and any relevant anatomic regions based on disease history, signs, and symptoms; MRI of abdomen and pelvis is also allowed) must be obtained after tumor resection for lifileucel generation and before any study therapy has been initiated, including NMA-LD. Baseline imaging will serve as the comparator for response evaluation. All areas of the tumor must be imaged at Baseline, and all participants with previously identified brain metastases at any point in their disease history or if clinically indicated must have brain imaging repeated. Baseline imaging must be completed
prior to the lifileucel infusion.
Assessment Period: The imaging modality and anatomic regions assessed must remain the same for all assessments while on study.
End-of-Assessment Visit: Subsequent anticancer therapy should only be started after documentation of progressive disease (PD). If new therapy must be started urgently, radiographic assessments for the End-of-Assessment should be performed as soon as possible  after the first dose.
High-resolution CT with contrast or contrast-enhanced MRI is the preferred imaging modality for assessing radiographic tumor response. Positron emission tomography (PET)-CT is encouraged, especially in cases where all known disease is not well visualized on CT, but not required. If a participant has a known allergy to CT contrast material, please use the alternate modality. In cases where contrast is strictly contraindicated, a noncontrast scan is acceptable. Please refer to the Imaging Guidelines for scanning options and a comprehensive list of scanning modalities in decreasing order of preference.
Additional radiologic assessments may be performed per investigator’s discretion. All imaging assessments, including scheduled and unscheduled scans, except images that were not done for the purpose of tumor assessment, must be recorded in the eCRF by the investigator or designee. Tumor response assessments should be evaluated preferably by a single, designated radiologist, for consistency, or by the study investigator or designee, and documented by a qualified investigator participating in the study.
The same method of assessment (CT or MRI) and the same technique for acquisition of data should be used unless contraindicated (eg, participants develop allergic reaction to contrast media during the course of the study) to characterize each identified and reported lesion at Baseline and at all post-treatment tumor assessments (scheduled or unscheduled).
See the Imaging Guidelines for additional instructions.

Adverse Events of Special Interest
Uveitis
Uveitis, or inflammation of the uvea (which consists of the iris, ciliary body, and choroid), has been observed in participants who have received autologous TIL product for the treatment of metastatic melanoma and is considered an AESI. Uveitis in these participants was mild to moderate in intensity, associated with symptoms such as blurred vision, eye pain, nyctalopia, photopsia, retinal hemorrhage, and/or vitreous floaters, and often improved with corticosteroid therapy.
Uveitis may be caused by a number of different etiologies; therefore, diagnosis and treatment of the underlying disease are imperative not only to treat the disease but also to preserve vision and potentially uncover underlying systemic diseases. If untreated or not appropriately treated, acute inflammation can develop into chronic, sight-threatening inflammation. Thus, when a participant presents with clinical signs and symptoms that may be associated with uveitis (eg, blurry vision; dull, aching, or throbbing pain in the temple or periorbital region; photophobia; circumlimbal injection; anterior chamber cells and flare; altered intraocular pressure), the investigator should refer the participant to a trained eye specialist for further evaluation. Once an etiology has been established, appropriate treatment should be commenced and customized to each participant as suboptimal treatment may lead to complications and loss of vision.
All AEs and SAEs of uveitis must be recorded on the AE eCRF for transmission to the sponsor’s safety database within 24 hours, even if they are considered nonserious according to the usual regulatory criteria. The investigator must follow any participant experiencing uveitis until resolution or stabilization of the event, an alternative explanation other than study intervention is provided for the event, or the participant is lost to follow-up. The investigator should record on the AE eCRF any further details obtained from follow-up for transmission to the sponsor’s safety database within 24 hours of the follow-up details becoming available.

Health-related Quality of Life Assessments
Patient-reported outcomes for HRQoL will be assessed using the EuroQol Research Foundation’s EQ-5D-5L™ (Section 10.8) and the EORTC’s QLQ-C30 (Section 10.9) instruments and each will be analyzed per the published evaluation manuals. The participant should complete the EQ-5D-5L first then the QLQ-C30; the questionnaires should be completed before any other procedures commence at the visits specified in the Schedule of Activities (Section 1.3). If a questionnaire is not completed at a scheduled visit, the reason will be documented on the eCRF and in the source documents.
The EQ-5D-5L instrument includes 5 dimensions (mobility, self-care, usual activities, pain/discomfort and anxiety/depression) and has 5 response levels for each: no problems, slight problems, moderate problems, severe problems, unable to/extreme problems (Herdman 2011). The questionnaire also records the participant’s overall current health on a vertical visual analogue scale (VAS) with the endpoints labelled ‘The best health you can imagine’ and ‘The
worst health you can imagine’. The VAS provides a quantitative measure of the participant’s perception of their overall health.
The QLQ-C30 instrument is composed of multi-item scales and single-item measures. It includes 5 functional scales (physical, role, emotional, cognitive, and social), 3 symptom scales (fatigue, nausea/vomiting, and pain), a global health status/QoL scale, and 6 single items (dyspnea, insomnia, appetite, constipation, diarrhea, and financial difficulties). Each of the scales and single-item measures have a score that ranges from 0 to 100, with a higher score on functional scales indicating better function and a higher score on symptom scales indicating worse symptoms (Aaronson 1993). Baseline scores, post baseline scores and change from baseline will be descriptively tabulated (number, mean, standard deviation, median, min, max) at each time point.
STATISTICAL CONSIDERATIONS
The statistical analysis plan (SAP) will be finalized prior to database lock and it will include a more technical and detailed description of the statistical analyses described in this section. This section is a summary of the planned statistical analyses of the most important endpoints including primary and key secondary endpoints.
The statistical analysis will be based on the estimation of efficacy and safety parameters and will be performed for the primary population as well as the overall population unless otherwise specified (see the definitions of these populations in Section 3).
Statistical Hypotheses and Timing of Analysis
At the time of primary analysis, the hypothesis testing of the primary efficacy endpoint, ORR per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the IRC, will be performed as following:
Primary Hypothesis (H1): H01: ORR ≤10% vs. Ha1: ORR >10% in the primary population
Secondary Hypothesis (H2): H02: ORR ≤10% vs. Ha2: ORR >10% in the overall population
The H1 null hypothesis will be tested at a significance level of 5% (two-sided). The hypothesis will be rejected if the lower bound of the two-sided 95% Clopper-Pearson CI of the ORR is greater than 10%. The study is considered to have met its primary objective if null hypothesis H01 is rejected.
If the null hypothesis H01 is rejected, a secondary hypothesis testing of H2 will be performed based on the overall population. Similarly, H2 will be tested at a significance level of 5% (two-sided). The null hypothesis H02 will be rejected if the lower bound of the two-sided 95% Clopper-Pearson CI of ORR in the overall population is greater than 10%.
The null hypothesis H02 will only be tested after the null hypothesis H01 is rejected. This fixed sequence testing procedure is used to ensure strong control of the family-wise error rate at a two-sided significance level of 5%.
The statistical hypothesis for the primary efficacy endpoint will be tested only once at the primary statistical analysis. The primary analysis and statistical hypothesis testing of primary efficacy endpoint will be performed after all confirmed responders in the primary population have had  follow up from their initial response.
The final analysis will be performed after all participants have been infused and their OS has been followed to maturity, or until at least 70% of participants have died, in order to characterize the effects on long term outcome data for DOR, PFS, and OS. The primary efficacy endpoint might be summarized descriptively based on a different data cut after the primary statistical analysis but not for statistical hypothesis testing.
Analysis Sets
For the purpose of analysis, the following analysis sets are defined.
The Safety Population will be the primary set for the analysis of safety data. The FAS will be the primary set for the analysis of efficacy data. Additional analyses of AE data will be conducted using the Safety Analysis Set. The ORR and AE analyses will also be performed using the TR Set.
Statistical Analyses
General Considerations
This section describes the statistical methods to address primary and secondary objectives. Methods related to exploratory objectives will be described in the SAP.
Primary Efficacy Endpoints Analysis
The primary endpoint is ORR, defined as the proportion of participants who have a confirmed CR or PR per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the IRC, from the date of lifileucel infusion until disease progression, start of a new anticancer therapy, or death due to any cause, whichever occurs first. A participant without any baseline or any postbaseline tumor measurements is considered as a non-responder. The ORR will be summarized using point estimates and 2-sided 95% CIs based on the Clopper-Pearson exact method.
Secondary Efficacy Endpoints Analysis
The CR rate and the DCR will be summarized using point estimates and 2-sided 95% CIs based on the Clopper-Pearson exact method.
CR rate is defined as the proportion of participants who have a confirmed CR per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the IRC from the date of lifileucel infusion until disease progression, start of a new anticancer therapy, or death due to any cause, whichever occurs first.
DCR is defined as the proportion of participants with a best overall confirmed response of CR or PR at any time or SD  per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the IRC from the date of lifileucel infusion until disease progression, start of new anticancer therapy, or death due to any cause, whichever occurs first.
Kaplan-Meier methods will be used to summarize time-to-event efficacy endpoints. Median time to event will be reported along with the 2-sided 95% CI.
DOR analyses will be performed for participants who achieve confirmed CR or PR per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the IRC. DOR is measured from the first time that
criteria are met for CR or PR until disease progression or death due to any cause. Participants not experiencing PD or who have not died prior to the time of data cut will have their event times censored at the last adequate tumor assessment. For participants who received new anticancer therapies, DOR will be censored at the date of last tumor response assessment prior to the start of new anticancer therapies. For participants with PD or death immediately after two or more consecutive missing tumor assessment visits, the DOR will be censored at the last adequate tumor assessment prior to the missing tumor assessments.
PFS is defined as the time from the date of lifileucel infusion until disease progression per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the IRC or death due to any cause. Participants not experiencing PD or not having died at the time of the data cut will have their event times censored at the last adequate tumor assessment. For participants who received new anticancer therapy, the PFS will be censored at the date of the last tumor response assessment prior to the start of new anticancer therapies. For participants with PD or death immediately after two or more consecutive missing tumor assessment visits, the PFS will be censored at the last adequate tumor assessment prior to the missing tumor assessments.
OS is the time from the date of lifileucel infusion to death due to any cause. Participants not having died at the time of data cut will have their event times censored on the last date of their known survival status.
The secondary endpoints of ORR, CR rate, DCR, DOR, and PFS per RECIST v1.1 as assessed by the investigator will be analyzed in the same fashion as described above for the corresponding endpoints assessed by IRC.
Efficacy of BRAF Inhibitor (± MEK inhibitor) Following the Lifileucel Regimen
ORR, BOR, DOR, and PFS following the date of the first dose of BRAF inhibitor (± MEK inhibitor) will be analyzed for participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and not previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± MEK inhibitor) and received BRAF inhibitor (± MEK inhibitor) as the first subsequent anticancer therapy after the lifileucel regimen.
Safety Analyses
The safety of lifileucel will be characterized by the severity, seriousness, relationship to study intervention, and characteristics of REAEs and TEAEs, including SAEs, study intervention-related AEs, and AEs leading to early discontinuation of study intervention or death. These analyses will be descriptive based on participant incidence counts and their related percentages. REAEs are defined as AEs that occur from the first dose of the TIL regimen (ie, NMA-LD, TIL, or aldesleukin) and  after the last dose of the TIL regimen, or up to the start of a new anti-cancer therapy, whichever is earlier, which will be summarized using Safety Population. TEAEs are defined as AEs that occur from the start of lifileucel infusion and up to
after lifileucel infusion which will be summarized based on Safety Analysis Set. AEs will be coded using the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA). The severity of each AE will be graded by investigators using the NCI-CTCAE. Related AEs are based on the causal relationship to the study intervention reported by investigators.
An additional AE summary will include AEs that occur from tumor resection and up to
after tumor resection (enrolled but not treated participants) or up to the first dose of NMA-LD based on the TR Set.
Other safety analyses based on the summarization of vital signs and clinical laboratory tests will be descriptive and summarized using the Safety Population.
Other Analyses
Participant disposition will be summarized using frequency and percentage for each of the categories listed below for participants in primary population as well as overall population.
All screened participants and the reason for not enrolling
TR Set (Enrolled Set) and the associated reason for participants who did not receive any component of the lifileucel regimen
Safety Population and the associated reason for participants who did not receive lifileucel
Safety Analysis Set and the associated reason for participants who were excluded from the FAS
FAS and the associated reason for participants who discontinued from the assessment period or from the study, respectively
A summary will also be provided for the number and percentage of participants whose melanoma is BRAF V600 mutation positive and not previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± MEK inhibitor) but subsequently received a BRAF inhibitor (± MEK inhibitor) as the first anticancer therapy after the lifileucel regimen.
Baseline demographic and clinical characteristics will be summarized descriptively. Continuous variables will be summarized by number of participants, mean, standard deviation, median, minimum, and maximum values. Categorical variables will be summarized by number and percentage of participants in each category.

No interim analysis is planned for this study.
Sample Size Determination
Approximately 75 participants are planned to be infused with lifileucel in the primary population with approximately 100 participants in the overall population. The prospectively defined hypothesis H1 and H2 (as defined in Section 9.1) will be tested sequentially for the primary endpoint of ORR.
At a two-sided overall significance level of 0.05 using the exact test, for H1, a sample size of 75 participants will provide approximately 99% power to reject null hypothesis of 10% ORR assuming the true ORR for lifileucel is 31% in participants in primary population (Chesney
2022); similarly for H2, a sample size of 100 will provide more than 99% power to reject the null hypothesis of 10% ORR assuming the true ORR for lifileucel is 31% in the overall population.
The study is well-powered at 99% or higher to provide a precise point estimate of ORR in this single-arm study.

The voting members of the IDMC are external to the sponsor, must not be study investigators, and must have no competing interests that could affect their roles regarding the study.
The IDMC will review the study data when up to a total of 25 participants whose melanoma is either BRAF V600 mutation positive and previously treated with a BRAF inhibitor (± a MEK inhibitor) or is BRAF V600 wild-type (ie, the primary population) have completed  post lifileucel infusion. Enrollment will continue while the data are under review. The IDMC will also review the study data after up to a total of 50 participants in primary population have completed
post lifileucel infusion.
Specific details concerning IDMC data review procedures are specified in the IDMC charter.
Independent Review Committee (IRC)
Tumor responses will be assessed by the IRC using RECIST v1.1 to ensure independent verification of efficacy assessments. All locally-obtained images obtained for tumor assessment will be forwarded to a central imaging facility for the IRC assessment of tumor responses.
Specific details concerning IRC procedures are specified in the IRC charter.
Dissemination of Clinical Study Data
The study information and results of this study will be posted on the US National Institute of Health’s website (www.clinicaltrials.gov) the Clinical Trials Information System (CTIS) in Europe, and any other registries required per local regulations.
Data Quality Assurance
All participant data relating to the study will be recorded on case report forms (CRFs) unless transmitted to the sponsor or designee electronically (eg, laboratory data). The investigator is responsible for verifying that data entries are accurate and correct by electronically signing the CRF.
Guidance on completion of CRFs will be provided in the eCRF Completion Guideline.
The investigator must permit study-related monitoring, audits, IRB/IEC review, and regulatory agency inspections and provide direct access to source documents.
Monitoring details describing strategy, including definition of study critical data items and processes (eg, risk-based initiatives in operations and quality such as risk
management and mitigation strategies and analytical risk-based monitoring), methods, responsibilities, and requirements, including handling of noncompliance issues and monitoring techniques (central, remote, or on-site monitoring) are provided in the Clinical Trial Agreement.
The sponsor or designee is responsible for the data management of this study, including quality checking of the data.
The sponsor assumes accountability for actions delegated to other individuals (eg, contract research organizations).
Records and documents, including signed ICFs, pertaining to the conduct of this study must be retained by the investigator until notified in writing by the sponsor that the trial-related records are no longer needed. No records may be transferred to another location or party without written notification to the sponsor.
Source Documents
Source documents provide evidence for the existence of the participant and substantiate the integrity of the data collected in the CRFs. Source documents are filed at the investigator’s site.
Data reported on the CRF or entered in the eCRF that are transcribed from source documents must be consistent with the source documents or the discrepancies must be explained. The investigator may need to request previous medical records or transfer records, depending on the study. Also, current medical records must be available.
Definition of what constitutes source data and its origin can be found in the monitoring guidelines, which are maintained by the sponsor.
The investigator must maintain accurate documentation (source data) that supports the information entered in the CRF.
The sponsor or designee will perform monitoring to confirm that data entered into the CRF by authorized site personnel are accurate, complete, and verifiable from source documents; that the safety and rights of participants are being protected; and that the study is being conducted in accordance with the currently approved protocol and any other study agreements, ICH GCP, and all applicable regulatory requirements.
Study and Site Start and Closure Study Start Date
The study start date is the earliest date that informed consent was provided among participants who enroll in this study.
Study/Site Termination
The sponsor or designee reserves the right to close the study site or terminate the study at any time for any reason at the sole discretion of the sponsor. Study sites will be closed upon study completion. A study site is considered closed when all required documents and study supplies have been collected and a study-site closure visit has been performed.
The investigator may initiate study-site closure at any time, provided there is reasonable cause and sufficient notice is given in advance of the intended termination. Reasons for the early closure of a study site by the sponsor or investigator may include but are not limited to:
For study termination:
Discontinuation of further lifileucel development For site termination:
Failure of the investigator to comply with the protocol, the requirements of the IRB/IEC or local health authorities, the sponsor’s procedures, or GCP guidelines
Inadequate or no recruitment (evaluated after a reasonable amount of time) of participants by the investigator
Total number of participants included earlier than expected
If the study is prematurely terminated or suspended, the sponsor shall promptly inform the investigators, the IECs/IRBs, the regulatory authorities, and any contract research organization(s) used in the study of the reason for termination or suspension, as specified by the applicable regulatory requirements. The investigators shall promptly inform the participants and should assure appropriate participant therapy and/or follow-up.
Publication Policy
The sponsor will comply with the requirements for publication of study results. The sponsor will be responsible for determining when the study results should be published. The investigator shall not submit a publication or abstract to journals or professional societies without the prior written approval of the sponsor, except as permitted by the agreed terms of the Clinical Trial Agreement, including after the reporting of the results of this study by the sponsor and other institutions.
In accordance with standard editorial and ethical practice, the sponsor will generally support publication of multicenter studies only in their entirety and not as individual site data. In this case, a coordinating investigator will be designated by mutual agreement.
Authorship will be determined by mutual agreement and in line with International Committee of Medical Journal Editors authorship requirements.

Appendix 3: AEs and SAEs: Definitions and Procedures for Recording, Evaluating, Follow-up, and Reporting
Definition of AE
Definition of SAE
NOTE: Participants may require hospitalization as per institutional guidelines (tumor resection, NMA-LD preparative regimen, and treatment recovery). However, if the institutional guidelines mandate a required hospitalization longer than the protocol, this preplanned hospitalization will not be considered an SAE.
Recording and Follow-up of an AE/SAE/AESI
Reporting of SAEs to the Sponsor
Special Situations Reporting
Definitions of Special Situations
Special situation reports include reports of medication error, overdose, AEs associated with product complaints, occupational exposure, and pregnancy reports regardless of an associated AE. The special situation reports will be reported in the AE eCRF but not considered an AE/SAE unless associated with an AE/SAE. Any AE resulting from a special situation should be reported on the AE eCRF.
Medication error is any unintentional error in the prescribing, dispensing, or administration of a medicinal/investigational product while in the control of the health care provider, participant, or consumer.
An overdose is defined as an accidental or intentional administration of a quantity of a medicinal/investigational product given per administration or cumulatively that is above the maximum recommended dose as per protocol or in the product labeling (as it applies to the daily dose of the participant in question).
Product complaint is defined as complaints arising from potential deviations in the manufacture, packaging, or distribution of the medicinal/investigational product.
Occupational exposure is defined as the exposure to a medicinal product as a result of one’s professional or nonprofessional occupation.
Procedures for Pregnancy Reporting: Maternal Exposure
If a participant becomes pregnant during the study, the investigational products should be discontinued immediately if not already infused.
Pregnancy itself is not regarded as an AE unless there is a suspicion that the investigational product(s) under study may have interfered with the effectiveness of a contraceptive medication. Congenital abnormalities or birth defects and spontaneous miscarriages should be reported and handled as SAEs and recorded on the AE eCRF. Elective abortions without complications should not be handled as AEs. The outcome of all pregnancies (spontaneous miscarriage, elective termination, ectopic pregnancy, normal birth, or congenital abnormality) should be followed up and documented even if the participant was discontinued from the study.
If any pregnancy occurs during the study, the investigator or other site personnel should inform the appropriate Iovance or designated representatives within	of when he or she becomes aware of it.
For participants who received a lifileucel infusion, any pregnancy that occurs while on the study through  from the last dose of aldesleukin or until the first dose of the subsequent anticancer therapy, whichever is longer, must be reported using the Pregnancy Report form within  of becoming aware of the pregnancy. A spontaneous abortion is always considered to be an SAE and will be reported on the AE eCRF as described in Section 10.3.4.
The participant should receive appropriate monitoring and care until the conclusion of the pregnancy to determine the outcome and status of the participant and child. The outcome should be reported to the sponsor or designee using the Pregnancy Outcome form. Any SAE occurring in association with a pregnancy, brought to the investigator’s attention after the participant has completed the study treatment and assessment period visits, must be promptly reported to the sponsor or their representative.
The same timelines apply when outcome information is available.
Pregnancy Report forms and Pregnancy Outcome forms should be emailed to:
Other Special Situation Reporting
All other special situation reports involving the study treatment must be recorded on the AE eCRF and transmitted to the sponsor’s safety database within 24 hours of becoming aware of the situation. Special situations involving concomitant medications do not need to be reported; however, any AE resulting from a special situation should be reported on the AE eCRF page.
